summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Michael Abrash's 'Graphics Programming Black Book' and uses it to illustrate a fundamental truth about code optimization: there's no such thing as the fastest possible code. Through Abrash's anecdotes about word-counting program challenges, the post shows how even world-class optimizers can achieve unexpected speedups once they abandon the assumption that their code is already optimal. The post argues that the biggest barrier to performance isn't technical limitations but programmer mindset—believing you've already found the best solution prevents you from discovering faster approaches. Abrash's challenges resulted in order-of-magnitude improvements over already-optimized assembly code, proving that creative thinking from multiple perspectives consistently beats assumed perfection.
key_points: The belief that you have the fastest possible code is itself the biggest optimization barrier
A brilliant teacher makes any technical subject compelling, regardless of how dated or specialized it seems
Terje Mathisen expected 5% improvements but achieved near-doubling once he opened his mind to new approaches
Community challenges on Abrash's optimized assembly achieved 3x speedups, nearly an order of magnitude over the original C
Technical skill without humility about the limits of your own solutions caps your potential for improvement
money_quotes: There ain't no such thing as the fastest code.
The biggest optimization barrier that Terje faced was that he thought he had the fastest code possible.
The class topic doesn't matter when the instructor is a brilliant teacher.
Any code can be made faster than you'd expect, if you think about it long enough and from many different perspectives.
If you think there is, you're probably the barrier standing in the way of further performance, not the code itself.
The biggest problem of all – the one that sits behind the keyboard.
key_insight: The assumption that your code is already optimal is the primary obstacle to achieving genuinely optimal performance.
